alsoKnownAs: Giant Gourami (erroneous — distinct from Osphronemus goramy)

nativeRange.habitat: Still and slow-moving weedy freshwater: ponds, ditches, lakes, large rivers, rice fields, and estuaries with abundant marginal and submerged vegetation

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Vallisneria, Hygrophila, Cryptocoryne)
floating plants (Amazon frogbit, Indian fern) to diffuse light and support bubble nests
driftwood and leaf litter for tannin-stained, soft-water biotope
minimal surface agitation — sponge filter or gentle internal filter
dark substrate to reduce stress and enhance male coloration

diet.notes: Omnivorous and generally unfussy. Accepts high-quality tropical flakes, micro-pellets, and granules as a staple. Regular offerings of live and frozen foods — bloodworms, mosquito larvae, brine shrimp, daphnia, and tubifex — significantly improve coloration and conditioning for breeding. Algae wafers and blanched vegetables may be accepted. Wild fish feed on small invertebrates, zooplankton, algae, and plant matter.

goodWith: other small labyrinth fish of different genera
avoidWith: Thick-lipped Gourami (Colisa labiosa / Trichogaster labiosa) — hybridization risk
aggressive or boisterous fin-nippers (tiger barbs, serpae tetras)
multiple males in small tanks — territorial fighting
fast-moving or overly active tankmates that cause stress

summary: A vividly colored South Asian labyrinth fish, males display a striking orange body crossed with iridescent blue-green diagonal bands. Peaceful in community aquariums, though males are territorial with conspecifics. Uncommon in trade despite being beginner-friendly and beautiful.
careNotes: The Banded Gourami is one of the larger members of the Trichogaster genus, with males reaching up to 12.5 cm TL (FishBase) though the typical aquarium-kept fish is closer to 10 cm. A tank of at least 70 litres with a 75 cm footprint (SeriouslyFish recommendation: 75 cm × 30 cm × 30 cm) is adequate for a single pair; a longer tank of 90 cm or more is advisable if attempting to keep more than one male, as males become territorial, especially during breeding. Provide a heavily planted setup with floating plants (Amazon frogbit, Indian fern) to diffuse light and provide cover — the species naturally inhabits still, weedy, dimly lit waters and becomes stressed under strong lighting or surface turbulence. A sponge or gentle internal filter is preferred; powerful canister filters should be baffled. Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 5–15, kH 2–8) and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5); softer, slightly acidic water mimics the species' native floodplain habitat and is particularly important for successful breeding. Temperature tolerance is broad (21–28°C). Like all labyrinth fish, the Banded Gourami must have unobstructed access to the water surface to breathe atmospheric air — never cover the surface completely and ensure the air above the water is not significantly colder than the tank. Males are substantially more colorful than females, displaying vibrant orange flanks crossed with oblique iridescent blue-green stripes; females are dull grayish-olive with rounded fins. Females should not be kept alone with a male in small tanks — the male can relentlessly harass a female that is not ready to spawn. Note: the colloquial name 'Giant Gourami' is occasionally misapplied to this species in local Asian markets; it is an entirely different species from the true Giant Gourami (Osphronemus goramy), which can reach 70 cm. T. fasciata was formerly placed in the genus Colisa and is still encountered under the synonym Colisa fasciata in older literature and some trade sources.
breedingNotes: Bubble-nest builder. Males construct a floating nest of bubbles and plant debris at the surface, typically among floating plants or beneath a broad leaf. Condition both sexes on live and frozen foods for 1–2 weeks before introducing to a dedicated breeding tank (minimum 60 litres, soft slightly acidic water, temperature raised to 26–28°C, 10–15 cm water depth, abundant floating plants). The male displays intensely to the female; spawning occurs beneath the nest in a typical gourami embrace. Females can produce 300–1,000 eggs across multiple spawnings during a single breeding event. After spawning, remove the female to prevent male aggression — the male alone tends the nest and guards the eggs. Eggs hatch in 24–36 hours; fry become free-swimming in approximately 3 days. First foods: infusoria and paramecia; progress to baby brine shrimp nauplii, microworms, and vinegar eels within the first week. The male should be removed once fry are free-swimming to prevent predation. Natural breeding season in the wild is March–September. Breeding in captivity is considered moderately straightforward compared to other labyrinth fish.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Trichogaster fasciata
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided: adult size (males to ~10 cm), temperature 21–28°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 5–15°H, minimum tank dimensions 75 × 30 × 30 cm (71 L), omnivorous diet, native range (Pakistan/India/Nepal/Bangladesh/Myanmar), breeding egg count 500–1,000, hatch time 24–36 h, hybridization warning with Colisa labiosa. Also noted species uncommon in trade and popular as food fish in native range.

additional.name: FishBase — Trichogaster fasciata
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum adult size (12.5 cm TL — higher than SeriouslyFish 10 cm; 12.5 cm used as the maximum for adultSizeCm.max), temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 4–15 dH, native range and habitat (large rivers, estuaries, ditches, ponds, lakes — weedy environments), taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes), trophic level 2.8 (omnivore), conservation status Least Concern, obligate air-breathing confirmed.

additional.name: Aquadiction — Banded Gourami care profile
additional.notes: Supplementary trade-oriented source. Confirmed lifespan 4–6 years, GH 5–15, temperature 23–28°C, diet (flakes/granules plus bloodworms/mosquito larvae/brine shrimp/daphnia/algae wafers), peaceful temperament with male territoriality during breeding, egg count 300–800, compatibility (barbs/danios/rasboras/loaches/catfish), avoid thick-lipped gourami (hybridization). Sexual dimorphism: males with iridescent orange-and-blue banded coloration vs. dull females.

fish.breedingNotes: Bubble-nest builder; breed readily when conditioned on live/frozen foods in a separate tank with soft acidic water, floating plants, and water depth reduced to 10–15 cm. Remove female post-spawn to prevent male aggression. Fry require infusoria initially. Natural breeding season March–September.
